skill.name: mmx-cli
skill.description: Use mmx to create text, images, video, speech, music, web search results, and MiniMax API resource outputs from a terminal.
skill.summary: Run MiniMax multimodal generation and API utility commands from an agent-friendly CLI guide.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are Markdown command examples and CLI flag names rather than executable skill code. Real risks remain because the guide instructs agents to install and run a third-party CLI, send user content and local files to MiniMax APIs, and manage API keys in files or environment variables.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party CLI Execution and Network API Calls
medium_findings.description: The guide tells agents to install mmx-cli and run many mmx commands that call the MiniMax platform. This is expected behavior, but it can transmit prompts, files, generated assets, and search queries to an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly install and invoke a CLI for MiniMax API functions. The intent is legitimate, but external service use is clear and user data may leave the local environment.

medium_findings.title: API Key Storage and Environment Variable Use
medium_findings.description: The documentation stores credentials under ~/.mmx/credentials.json and also supports MINIMAX_API_KEY. This is not malicious, but agents must avoid exposing the key in logs, command history, or shared outputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential file path and API key environment variable are explicitly documented. This is a normal auth pattern, but it creates clear secret-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Local File Reads and Writes for Media Workflows
medium_findings.description: Several commands read local files or write generated images, videos, speech, and music. Agents should only use user-approved paths because private files can be uploaded or overwritten.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill documents path-based inputs and output paths across media commands. These operations are legitimate but require path controls and consent.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Most reported backticks are Markdown inline code, command examples, model names, or fenced code blocks. The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear to match strings in documentation, not cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the referenced lines shows documentation syntax and CLI examples rather than Ruby execution or cryptographic code. No executable Ruby or crypto logic was found in the only scanned file.

low_findings.title: Shell Substitution Examples Need Normal Command Hygiene
low_findings.description: The piping examples use shell substitution and jq. These are normal examples, but copied commands should not be combined with untrusted prompts, paths, or task identifiers without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell substitutions are visible and are used to chain mmx outputs into later commands. They are not malicious, but they can amplify mistakes in agent-run shells.

dangerous_patterns.title: Global Package Installation Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The prerequisite section asks users to install a global npm package. This requires trust in the package source and can affect the host environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and operational. The risk depends on package integrity rather than malicious content in this skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential File in Home Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide documents persistent credentials in a hidden home-directory path. This increases impact if local file permissions or logs expose the path or key material.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential path is directly stated in the documentation. It is a legitimate pattern but should be treated as sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Callback URL Option
dangerous_patterns.description: The video command supports a callback URL. A malicious or mistaken URL could disclose task metadata to an unintended endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The callback option is documented but no concrete endpoint is present. The risk is contextual and depends on user-supplied values.

content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to create AI media and query MiniMax models from terminal workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise guide for using mmx commands safely and consistently.
actual_capabilities: Run MiniMax text chat commands with messages, system prompts, model options, and JSON output.
Generate images, videos, speech, and music through documented mmx command groups.
Describe images with MiniMax vision by using a local path, URL, or uploaded file ID.
Query MiniMax web search and quota commands from a terminal workflow.
Export mmx command schemas for use as agent tool definitions.
Use non-interactive flags for cleaner automation in agents and CI jobs.
limitations: Requires the external mmx-cli package and a valid MiniMax API key.
Generated media quality, latency, and quota use depend on MiniMax services.
Commands may upload prompts, local files, images, audio, or task data to MiniMax APIs.
The skill is documentation only and does not verify package integrity or API responses.

use_cases.title: Prototype campaign assets
use_cases.description: Create draft images, videos, narration, and music from one terminal workflow.

use_cases.title: Automate multimodal generation
use_cases.description: Use non-interactive mmx commands in scripts that need structured outputs and predictable exit codes.

use_cases.title: Inspect and describe media
use_cases.description: Ask MiniMax vision questions about local images or URLs during content review.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a simple image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use mmx to generate one image for this prompt: [describe the image]. Return the saved path or image URL only.

prompt_templates.title: Create speech from copy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use mmx speech synthesis for this text: [paste copy]. Save an MP3 file and tell me the output path.

prompt_templates.title: Build a video task workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Start an async mmx video generation task for [video idea], check task status, and download the completed file when ready.

prompt_templates.title: Export tool schemas for an agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Export the mmx tool schema for [command group], explain the available arguments, and identify which arguments need user approval.

output_examples.input: Generate a square product image for a stainless steel travel mug.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns the image location, generation settings used, and a short note about any missing prompt details.

output_examples.input: Create narration audio from a 90-word launch announcement.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns the saved audio path, selected voice, format, and duration when available.

output_examples.input: Describe a local photo and identify visible brand risks.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes the image contents, flags visible concerns, and avoids exposing unrelated local files.

best_practices: Use non-interactive, quiet, and JSON output flags when running commands through an agent.
Confirm file paths, callback URLs, and upload inputs with the user before execution.
Store API keys securely and avoid printing credentials in prompts, logs, or command output.
anti_patterns: Do not pass sensitive documents, private media, or secrets to MiniMax commands without explicit approval.
Do not use broad output directories or overwrite existing files without checking the target path.
Do not copy shell substitutions into production scripts without validating each input and output.

faq.question: Does this skill include executable code?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide for running the external mmx CLI.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: A MiniMax API key is required through mmx authentication, a command flag, or an environment variable.

faq.question: Can it generate more than text?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide covers text, images, video, speech, music, vision, search, quota, and schema export commands.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: Yes. mmx commands use MiniMax APIs, so prompts and selected files may leave the local environment.

faq.question: Is it suitable for automated agents?
faq.answer: Yes, if agents use non-interactive flags, explicit paths, and user-approved inputs.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The file is documentation, but it guides agents to run external commands, handle API keys, and upload local content.
